brief_document.title: aion-client-typescript (npm): Client, handle, generic typed payloads, AsyncIterable stream, error union
blocked_by: AW: aion-server API + aion-proto wire contract must be defined
brief_document.purpose: Build the TypeScript/Node caller SDK so JS-ecosystem teams and frontends-with-a-backend can drive Aion workflows with full type-checking. It implements the AL-001 contract in TS idioms: a connect-and-operate Client, a WorkflowHandle, generic JSON-typed payloads with a raw escape hatch, an AsyncIterable event stream that resumes across disconnects, and a branchable error type. tsc strict and eslint clean.
brief_document.task: Create the aion-client-typescript package (npm name aion-client-typescript). Implement a Client that connects to an aion-server (auth + TLS), exposes start/signal/query/cancel/list/describe with start returning a WorkflowHandle, generic typed payload helpers with a raw escape hatch, a subscribe AsyncIterable with sequence-based resumption, and an error union / class hierarchy over the shared taxonomy. Behaviour must match the AL-001 contract; the conformance harness is AL-007, but the package must be drivable by it.

requirements.title: Package scaffold and Client connection
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL create the package with a Client that connects to an aion-server with an auth credential and TLS configuration and exposes start, signal, query, cancel, list, and describe, with start returning a WorkflowHandle. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT require reconnection per operation.
acceptance: sdks/typescript/aion-client/package.json declares name aion-client-typescript, the module/types entry points, and a strict tsconfig is present.
A connect(options) factory (or new Client(options)) with { endpoint, auth, tls } establishes a reusable client.
Client exposes async start, signal, query, cancel, list, describe matching the contract; start resolves to a WorkflowHandle.
WorkflowHandle exposes signal/query/cancel/describe/subscribe bound to workflowId and runId, defaulting to the latest run and targeting a specific run when given one.
A connection failure rejects with Unavailable; a rejected credential rejects with Unauthenticated.

requirements.title: Error union / class hierarchy
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL map server failures to a branchable error type covering the shared taxonomy. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT reject with a plain Error for a condition that has a specific variant and SHALL NOT swallow failures.
acceptance: errors.ts defines the taxonomy as a discriminated union (on a literal kind/code field) and/or an Error-subclass hierarchy with a common base: NotFound, AlreadyExists, QueryFailed, QueryTimeout, Cancelled, Unavailable, Unauthenticated, NamespaceDenied, InvalidArgument, Server (with detail).
A mapping function converts a server/transport failure into the correct variant; a unit test asserts the NotFound, AlreadyExists, QueryTimeout, and Unavailable mappings.
The discriminant is exhaustively switchable so a consumer's switch with no default still type-checks for all variants.
No catch block discards an error without re-throwing or converting it.

requirements.title: Generic typed payload helpers with raw escape hatch
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL make the operations generic over input/result types with JSON serialisation while keeping the wire Payload, and SHALL keep a raw-Payload escape hatch on every payload-bearing operation. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT remove the raw path.
acceptance: payload.ts provides toPayload<T>(value): Payload (JSON content-type) and fromPayload<T>(payload): T.
start<I>, signal<I>, and query<I, R> are generic over input/result types; raw overloads accept a Payload (bytes + content-type) directly.
A serialisation failure throws InvalidArgument; a result-decode failure throws a non-swallowed error.
A unit test round-trips a typed value through toPayload/fromPayload and asserts deep equality.
tsc --noEmit (strict) reports no errors for the payload surface.

requirements.title: subscribe AsyncIterable with resumption
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL implement subscribe returning an AsyncIterable of decoded events. WHILE open, IF the connection drops transiently, THEN THE SYSTEM SHALL reconnect and resume from the last delivered sequence number with no gaps or duplicates. IF the subscription fails terminally, THEN THE SYSTEM SHALL throw out of the iterator rather than completing silently.
acceptance: stream.ts exposes an AsyncIterable<Event> (for await (const event of handle.subscribe()) { ... }) over the WebSocket transport.
The iterator tracks the last delivered sequence number and resumes from sequence+1 after a transient disconnect.
A test with a stub transport that drops mid-stream asserts each event is yielded exactly once across the reconnect.
A terminal failure throws the mapped error out of the for-await loop; the iterable does not complete silently on failure.
tsc --noEmit (strict) reports no errors for the stream surface.

boundaries: SHALL NOT define the wire protocol or server endpoints (AW).
SHALL NOT depend on the Rust crate at runtime — it is an independent npm package over the same wire contract.
SHALL NOT implement the cross-language conformance harness — that is AL-007.
verification: The package's test runner passes on sdks/typescript/aion-client/test.
The build emits both runtime JS and .d.ts type declarations, and the public index re-exports the seven operations.
No catch block silently discards an error.

checklist.text: Client connects to an aion-server endpoint with auth + TLS config and exposes start, signal, query, cancel, list, and describe; start returns a WorkflowHandle with per-workflow methods.
Payload helpers are generic over input/result types with JSON serialisation and a raw-Payload escape hatch on every payload-bearing operation; the public API is typed while the wire stays Payload.

subscribe returns an AsyncIterable of decoded events that resumes from the last delivered sequence number across transient disconnects and rejects/throws on terminal failure rather than ending silently.
Errors are a discriminated union / class hierarchy mapping the shared taxonomy; tsc --noEmit (strict) and eslint pass.

stories.text: As an application developer, I want to add a client dependency, point it at a server URL, and start a workflow by type name with a typed input so that I can trigger durable work from my service without learning the engine internals.
As an application developer, I want start to return a handle that lets me signal, query, cancel, and describe the workflow I just started so that I do not have to plumb IDs through my own code.
As an application developer, I want a typed result back from a query and a typed input accepted by a signal so that my compiler catches payload-shape mistakes before runtime.

As a dashboard developer, I want to subscribe to a workflow's live event stream and have the client resume transparently across transient disconnects so that I render a gap-free, duplicate-free timeline without writing reconnection logic.
As a dashboard developer, I want a terminal stream failure to surface as an error through the stream rather than a silent end so that I can distinguish a finished subscription from a broken one.

constraints.text: Clients consume the aion-server API and aion-proto wire types; they SHALL NOT define their own wire formats, endpoints, or fields the server does not serve. The protocol is owned by AW.

Clients SHALL NOT depend on engine internals (aion, aion-store, beamr). The Rust client may depend on aion-core (shared domain types) and aion-proto; the embedded constructor may depend on aion only behind a feature flag, never by default.
All four SDKs expose the same seven operations with identical observable behaviour, verified by the shared conformance suite.
Every SDK exposes both a typed payload surface (per language) and a raw-Payload escape hatch; neither is removable.
Every SDK maps server failures to a documented, branchable error taxonomy. No swallowed errors, no single catch-all collapsing distinct failures.

subscribe resumes transparently across transient disconnects using the per-event sequence number, delivering a gap-free duplicate-free stream; terminal failures surface through the stream, not as a silent end.
start honours a caller-supplied idempotency key: a retried identical start returns the original handle; a conflicting reuse raises AlreadyExists.

Per-ecosystem toolchain gates pass: Rust (clippy -D warnings, fmt --check, no allow/expect/ignore), Python (ruff + mypy --strict + pytest), TypeScript (tsc strict + eslint + tests), Gleam (gleam format --check + gleam check + gleam test). No-partial-implementation and no-silent-failure standards hold across all four.
Each package is publishable to its registry (crates.io, PyPI, npm, Hex) with a README, a type-checked public surface, and a runnable example covering all seven operations.

resolved_context.intention: Ship the caller-side SDKs for the workflow-caller role: aion-client (Rust, crates.io), aion-client-python (PyPI), aion-client-typescript (npm), and aion_client (Gleam, Hex). Each connects to an aion-server deployment and exposes the same seven operations — connect, start, signal, query, cancel, list, describe, subscribe — in the idioms of its language. A developer who has never read the engine internals should add a dependency, point at a server URL, and start a workflow with full type-checking in their language within minutes; a frontend developer using the TypeScript client should never see beamr, Gleam, or Rust. The four SDKs must behave identically where behaviour is observable, proven by a shared language-neutral behavioural contract and a conformance suite that exercises every SDK against a real server.
